coredata.dc:title: Angle-resolved one and Two-Photon absorption spectrum in twisted bilayer graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiangtao
Lu, Chen
Wang, Li
Wang, Jingang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, one-photon absorption (OPA) spectrum and two-photon absorption (TPA) spectrum and its electronic excitation, charge transfer, partial density of states (PDOS) and magnetic induction current density of zigzag edge twisted bilayer graphene quantum dots (Z-TwBLG-QDs) with different twisted angles which were divide into two pattern of Moiré superlatrate (MS) are qualitativly and quantitativly analyed by using density functional theory (DFT), excited state wave function, single electron approximation, and gauge-invariant atomic orbital (GIAO) method. The inter-layer local excitation in the electronic transition and intra-layer charge transfer dominated by MS and anti-symmetric electron-hole pair density induced by PDOS are discussed. The twisted angle is decisive to the absorption spectrum, large twisted angle will lead to the red shift of the absorption spectrum. The two-photon transition shows that the twisted angle can regulate the TPA cross section and the boundary effect of Z-TwBLG-QDs in a specific wavelength range. The edge effect of two-step transition can be enhanced by MS with specific twisted angles. The physical mechanism of twisted angle regulating electron transition behavior is revealed by magnetic induction current density analysis.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The physical mechanism of hole-electron anti-symmetric distribution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The left part is the UV–vis spectrum of Z-TwBLG-QDs with three different structures of MS, the left axis represents the molar absorption coefficient, the right axis represents the oscillator intensity of excited states (a, c and e), three combined Figure of integral curve (g). The right part is the TPA spectrum of Z-TwBLG-QDs with three different MS. The left axis represents the molar absorption coefficient, and the right axis is the TPA cross section (b, d and f), the combined Figure of TPA spectrum (h).

The excited state characteristics of three kinds of Z-TwBLG-QDs with different structures of MS near the main absorption peaks. The TDM and electron-hole pair density of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° at S15 (a). The TDM and electron-hole pair density of Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2° at S15 and S16 (b and c). The TDM and electron-hole pair density of Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2° at S19 (d).

The excited state characteristics of three kinds of Z-TwBLG-QDs with different structures of MS near the secondary absorption peaks. The TDM and electron-hole pair density of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° at S53 (a). The TDM and electron-hole pair density of Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2° at S53 (b). The TDM and electron-hole pair density of Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2° at S57 (c).

The second-contributing excited state characteristics of Z-TwBLG-QDs with D-D pattern structures of MS near the secondary absorption peaks. The TDM and electron-hole pair density of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° at S49 (a). The TDM and electron-hole pair density of Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2° at S49 (b).

The PDOS of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8°, Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2° and Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2°, fragment 1 to fragment 5 are represented by black, red, blue, green, and orange regions (a, b and c). The lower part is PDOS diagrams of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8°, Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2°, and Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2°, (d, e and f). The color of the integral curve is consistent with the fragment definition.

The Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8°, electron-hole pair isosurface and TDM of two step transition processes in S26 and S31 of TPA, which get through the intermediate states S15 (a-c) and S16 (d-e) The red and blue isosurface represent the electron and hole density.

Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2°, electron-hole pair isosurface and TDM of two step transition processes in S26 and S31 of TPA, which get through the intermediate states S16 (a-c) and S15 (d-e) The red and blue isosurface represent the electron and hole density, respectively.

Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2°, electron-hole pair isosurface and TDM of two step transition processes in S25 and S31 of TPA, which get through the intermediate states S20 (a-c) and S19 (d-e) The red and blue isosurface represent the electron and hole density.

The Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° electron-hole pair isosurface and TDM of two step transition processes in S40 and S42 of TPA, which get through the intermediate states S15 (a and b) and S16 (d-e). The red and blue isosurface represent the electron and hole density.

The Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2° electron-hole pair isosurface and TDM of two step transition processes in S40 and S42 of TPA, which get through the intermediate states S16 (a and b) and S15 (d and e). The red and blue isosurface represent the electron and hole density.

The Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2° electron-hole pair isosurface and TDM of two step transition processes in S38 and S54 of TPA, which get through the intermediate states S20 (a and b) and S19 (d and e). The red and blue isosurface represent the electron and hole density.

The induced current density (ring current) of Z-TwBLG-QDs with different twisted angles(27.8° (a), 32.2° (b) and 38.2° (c)) induced by the external magnetic field (The direction is perpendicular to the graphene plane from inside to outside, indicated by a green arrow) calculated by the ACID method, and the isovalue is 0.05. The yellow area represents the ring current isosurface, the green arrow with the smaller isosurface is the current density vector, and the vector sum represents the overall ring current direction of the system (red circle arrow curve).
The main two-photon transition process and transition dipole value of Z-TwBLG-QDs under different twisted angles.

head.ce:title: Angle-resolved one and Two-Photon absorption spectrum in twisted bilayer graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Liaoning Petrochemical University, Fushun 113001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Liaoning Petrochemical University, Fushun 113001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, one-photon absorption (OPA) spectrum and two-photon absorption (TPA) spectrum and its electronic excitation, charge transfer, partial density of states (PDOS) and magnetic induction current density of zigzag edge twisted bilayer graphene quantum dots (Z-TwBLG-QDs) with different twisted angles which were divide into two pattern of Moiré superlatrate (MS) are qualitativly and quantitativly analyed by using density functional theory (DFT), excited state wave function, single electron approximation, and gauge-invariant atomic orbital (GIAO) method. The inter-layer local excitation in the electronic transition and intra-layer charge transfer dominated by MS and anti-symmetric electron-hole pair density induced by PDOS are discussed. The twisted angle is decisive to the absorption spectrum, large twisted angle will lead to the red shift of the absorption spectrum. The two-photon transition shows that the twisted angle can regulate the TPA cross section and the boundary effect of Z-TwBLG-QDs in a specific wavelength range. The edge effect of two-step transition can be enhanced by MS with specific twisted angles. The physical mechanism of twisted angle regulating electron transition behavior is revealed by magnetic induction current density analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: The method of stacking two-dimensional materials in sequence to form new structures to achieve new functions provides a new means of artificial control of material properties[1-5]. In the process of preparing graphene, the laboratory produced bilayer graphene (BLG) with a certain twisted angle[6-8]. The twisted angle makes the honeycomb mesh lattice between the graphene layers produce long-period Moiré due to small dislocations, which determine the electronic properties of the BLG[9-12]. The interaction between the two misaligned layers significantly changes the low-energy band structure, flattening the band structure near the charge neutrality, and creating new electronic characteristics that are different from the inherent, such as band gaps, Van Hove singularities (VHS) appear in the far infrared region, interlayer electronics effects and many-body topological quantum states[13-17]. The relative angle between the two-dimensional crystal layer and the layer has a very important influence on the properties of the homogeneous material. For small twisted angles, the Moiré generated by the difference in crystal lattice orientation will produce long-range modulation, the twisted angle will also reduce the energy span between the TwBLG valence band and the conduction band, thereby changing the light absorption characteristics of the TwBLG[18-20]. Recently, energy band spectroscopy has studied the dependence of the photoconductivity of graphene layers on the stacking order. Optical emission spectroscopy with angular resolution provides a method to study the electronic properties of stacked TwBLG films with misaligned orientation angles[22,23]. TwBLG has many novel optical properties under the twisted angle, for example, the twisted angle is decisive for the optical conductivity. Under some twisted angles, the optical conductivity is independent of the optical frequency, and the absorption spectrum changes with the twisted angle[21,24,25]. However, the linear and nonlinear optical absorption properties of TwBLG controlled by different twisted angles have not been studied. The related physical mechanisms and principles of regulation need to be supplemented urgently.

In this work, we investigated the regulation mechanism of twisted angle on linear and nonlinear optical absorption properties of TwBLG, we designed Z-TwBLG-QDs based on different twisted angles by using zigzag edge graphene thin layer to study the physical mechanism of OPA, TPA and electron transition of TwBLG. TPA is a third-order nonlinear optical process in which the medium simultaneously absorbs two photons through an intermediate virtual state to transition from the ground state to the excited state, the quantum characteristics of the transition have high spatial resolution and good dielectric penetration[26]. Based on the high spatial selectivity and high permeability of materials in TPA process, organic nonlinear optical materials show excellent optical properties in TPA process, which greatly improves the application potential[27]. The TPA spectroscopy theory obtained by the TPA analysis program developed based on the theory of quadratic response is in good agreement with the experimental results, and is widely used in the calculation of nonlinear optical coefficients[28,29]. It is found that the electron transition property of TwBLG varies greatly with the energy level of the molecular orbital when the twisted angle changes. This variation can be observed by PDOS. Density of States (DOS) is a function of energy, which is different from the single-electron orbital obtained by solving the KS-DFT equation in first principles. In quantum chemical calculation, when using DOS to examine the electronic structure of the system, the state refers to the molecular orbitals calculated by HF, semi-empirical, KS-DFT and other theoretical methods based on the single-electron approximation. With the continuous improvement of the relevant quantization program, DOS can be used as a visual result of the band structure, which can well explain molecular orbital gap and analyze electron energy levels of some conjugated complexes in host–guest chemistry[30,31]. According to the above analysis, it can be found that the transition characteristics of twisted BLG with different twisted angles are significantly different under OPA and TPA. In order to reveal the physical mechanism of this difference, we used the anisotropy of the induced current density (AICD) function[32] to investigate the electron characteristics of the system. Essentially, AICD reflects the isotropic intensity of the induction of electrons in the corresponding position to the magnetic field, which is a scalar function in three-dimensional real space. The calculated magnetic induced current density can visually show the aromatosity of Z-TwBLG-QDs at different twisted angles, which is of great significance to reflect the movement behavior of free electrons in the system.

Firstly, perform geometric optimization processing on the BLG model, this process consists of two steps. The first step is to optimize the initial guess by combining GFN2-XTB (geometry, frequency, non-covalent, and extended close integration)[33] and Gaussian16[34], where the GFN2-XTB program is used for energy and gradient calculations, Gaussian16 is used for the construction of density matrix and the calculation of Hessian matrix. The information transmission of the two programs is realized through self-programming scripts. The second step uses density functional theory (DFT) method[35], B3LYP functional[36], 6-31G(d) basis set[37] and DFT-D3 correction to further geometry the structure completed in the first step optimization. TD-DFT calculation adopts TD-DFT, CAM-B3LYP functional[38], STO-3G basis set, keyword, IOp(9/40 = 4) is used to improve the accuracy of the configuration coefficient output by Gaussian 16. The process of TPA requires the calculation of transition dipole moments from ground state to intermediate state and from intermediate state to final state, including three-state and two-state models, sum-over-states (SOS) method and time-dependent perturbation theory to calculate the ground state and excited state wavefunctions and the transition dipole moments between them. The output result of electronic excitation is used to calculate the TPA transition, electron-hole pair density, and the transition density matrix (TDM) based on the SOS method. The electronic transition characteristic analysis and the local density of states are calculated by the wavefunction analysis program Multiwfn-3.7[39], the visualization of the charge transfer is drawn by the three-dimensional image processing program VMD-1.9.3[40].

When calculating DOS, quantum chemistry mainly studies isolated systems. It is different from the concept of
k
when calculating single electron orbital by solving the KS-DFT equation in first-principles. There is no concept of
k
when calculating DOS in quantum chemistry. The orbital energy levels that come out are all discretely distributed, as shown in formula (1):
(1)
TDOS
( E )
=
∑ i
δ
E -
ε i

Where
ε
is the orbital energy and
δ
is the Dirac function. The total density of states (TDOS) value is in the same interval in the energy level position of each molecular orbital. The TDOS graph is drawn as a pile of vertical lines. The DOS curve at this time obviously cannot examine the density of states of the isolated system. In order to intuitively analyze the density of orbit distribution in different energy regions, the widening function
F
( x )
was used to replace
δ
, such as using a Gaussian function, to get a widened TDOS.

The normalized Gaussian function is defined as:
G
( x )
=
1
c
2 π
e
-
x 2
2
x 2
(2)
c =
FWHM
2
2 ln 2

The larger the FWHM, the smoother the TDOS graph looks, which is more conducive to graphic analysis. This article focuses on the PDOS, which reflects the curve of the contribution of a specific segment to TDOS. If the fragment is properly defined, the essence and main composition of the orbitals of different energy intervals can be better grasped through the PDOS diagram. The PDOS of fragment
A
is defined as:
(3)
PDO
S A
( E )
=
∑ i
Θ
i , A
F
E -
ε i

Among the formula (3),
Θ
i , A
are the components of the fragment
A
in the orbital
i
.

In order to more conveniently study the induced current induced by the magnetic field to analyze molecular delocalization and aromaticity. Herges et al. defined the AICD function [32]:
(4)
Δ
T S
1
2
=
1 3
t
xx
-
t
yy
2
+
t
yy
-
t
zz
2
+
t
zz
-
t
xx
2
+
1 2
t
xy
+
t
yz
2
+
t
xz
+
t
zx
2
+
t
yz
+
t
zy
2

Where
t
is the current density tensor (3*3 matrix). It is different in disparate places in space, the dot product of the tensor and the external magnetic field vector is the induced current vector. This definition of induced current density anisotropy
( Δ
T S
( 1 )
2
)
is different from the conventional method of including off-diagonal elements of the current density tensor
(
t
nk
, n ≠ k )
. AICD reflects the isotropic intensity of the electrons in the corresponding position on the magnetic field. It is a scalar function in three-dimensional real space whose size is not directly related to the electron density, and it does not depend on the selection of the external field. Thus, it is very convenient to study the distribution by means of the isosurface in order to analyze electron delocalization and aromatic properties of the system. AICD calculation uses DFT method, PBEPBE[41] functional, Gaussian 16 combined with AICD2.0[42] program for NMR[43] calculation, keyword, NMR = CSGT is used to output current density tensor, weight and grid position information, this information is visualized in the renderer povray-2.7.

In order to study the regulation mechanism of the twisted angle on the linear and nonlinear optical properties of TwBLG. We designed Z-TwBLG-QDs which were formed by coupling two layers of parallel zigzag edge graphene nanosheets at a specific distance in the vertical direction. The procedure is to stack one serrated layer of graphene nanosheets on top of another. Then the top layer of graphene was twisted while keeping the underlying layer of graphene nanosheets in the same position, refer Fig. 1 (a), the twisted angles include 27.8°, 32.2°and 38.2°. Ultimately, we get three TwBLG models with different MS (Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8°, Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2°and Z-TwBLG-QDs-37.8°), we propose an intelligible way to classify MS, which define MS with the same shape but different rotation directions structure as D-D pattern, refer Fig. 1(b and c), and define different shapes structure of MS as D-S pattern, refer Fig. 1(b and d or c and d). Experiments have shown that Moiré is the origin of the new optical properties of Z-TwBLG-QDs. Firstly, during the formation of Moiré, the van der Waals force between BLG is weakened, thereby weakening the coupling between layers and changing the electronic properties of the zigzag edge. Secondly, Moiré will change the energy level of BLG, which will affect the electronic transition behavior[44].

The peak value of the absorption spectrum and the intensity of the oscillator are used to characterize the absorption intensity of the incident light. The intensity of the oscillator corresponds to the excited state, and is the basic physical quantity that describes the behavior of electronic transitions, refer to Table S1 of Supporting information. It can be seen from the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum (UV–vis) that the main absorption peak of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° is near 772.3 nm, and the absorption peak is mainly contributed by S15 with the largest oscillator intensity. The secondary peak is contributed by the two excited states S49 and S53, refer Fig. 2 (a). The main absorption peak of Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2° is near 772.4 nm which is contributed by two degenerate excited states S15 and S16 with the same oscillator intensity value. The secondary peak is also contributed by two excited states S49 and S53 whose oscillator intensity is almost same. UV–vis shows that Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° and Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2° have almost the same optical absorption characteristics, which is due to the same D-D pattern of MS produced by the two twisted angles. This also verifies that the optical properties of graphene sheets mainly depend on the edge structure, refer Fig. 2(b). Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2° has two absorption peaks. The main peak is contributed by S19 and S20, and the secondary peak is contributed by S57 and S58, refer Fig. 2(c). The combined figure shows that the spectral lines of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° and Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2° coincide, compared with the former two peaks, the main absorption peak of Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2° has a red shift, refer Fig. 2(g).

The right side of Fig. 2 shows the TPA spectrum of three different twisted angles and their combined figure When the twisted angle is 27.8° and 32.2°, the main absorption peak is all contributed by S26, and a shoulder peak is also included in the rising phase of the main absorption peak, which is all contributed by S31. The secondary absorption peak includes two excited states of S42 and S40. When the twisted angle is 38.2°, the main absorption peak is contributed by S25. The secondary absorption peak is dominated by S54, and the other shoulder peak is located in the descending stage of the secondary absorption peak and is contributed by S38. The right axis is the TPA cross section of the three systems, refer Fig. 2(b, d and f), which shows the intensity of TPA. The TPA is near the main absorption peak, and the TPA cross-section value is positively correlated with the twisted angle. Near the shoulder peak of the main absorption peak, the TPA cross sections of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° and Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2° are very close, but larger than Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2°. Near the main excited states of the secondary absorption peak, there are three value of the TPA cross section of the system does not change much, but near the shoulder peak, Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2° still has a certain degree of reduction compared with the two systems, refer to Table S2 of the Supporting information. It can be found that the TPA cross sections of the three systems are largely controlled by the twisted angle. The blue integral curve on the left axis of the TPA spectrum represents the two-step transition term in the two-photon transition process, the green integral curve represents the one-step transition term, and the black curve represents the sum of the TPA. Obviously, the total integral curve of the TPA of the three systems is completely contributed by the two-step transition term. Therefore, the main excited states are all two-step transitions. From the combined diagram of the TPA, with periodic complementary twisted angles of Z-TwBLG-QDs, refer to the black curve and red line in Fig. 2(g), their TPA spectrum coincide. When the twisted angle is 38.2°, the overall absorption spectrum is red-shifted, refer to the blue curve in Fig. 2(h).

In order to deeply analyze the electronic transition behavior of Z-TwBLG-QDs, the transition density matrix (TDM) and electron-hole pair density of excited states with large absorption intensity for incident light are drawn. TDM can well reflect the electronic transition characteristics of the system in the excited states between atoms and atomic groups, and the electron-hole pair density can visualize the positions of electrons and holes in the form of real space functions and ntuitively reflect the increase and decrease of the electron density from the ground state to the excited state. Fig. 3 (a) is the TDM of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° at S15, in which the two bright areas on the main diagonal represent the excitation characteristics of the bottom and top graphene respectively, the two sides of the non-main diagonal represent the charge transfer between layers. TDM shows that the transition density is located on the main diagonal, the bright areas are evenly distributed, there are weak transition densities on both sides, and there is no transition density on the non-main diagonal, so there is no charge transfer between layers. This indicates that S15 is a strong inter-layer localized excited state and intra-layer charge transfer excited state. From the electron-hole pair density distribution, the hole-electron is mainly distributed at the edge of Z-TwBLG-QDs, so the inter-layer localized excitation mainly comes from edge effect, refer Fig. 3(a), when the twisted angle change into 32.2°, the main excited states include the degenerate states S15 and S16. The TDM and electron-hole pair density of these two excited states are the same as the TDM and electron-hole pair density of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° in the main excited state. This is because the two systems have the same structure of MS and the same excitation characteristics. They are both local excitation of inter-layer and charge transfer excitation of intra-layer, refer Fig. 3(b and c). When the twisted angle is 38.2°, the transition density of the main excited state S19 on the Moiré is reduced compared to Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° and Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2°, electron-hole pair density distribution also reflects the significant reduction of the hole-electron isosurface on the Moiré, refer to Fig. 3(d). Therefore, S19 is still the inter-layer local excited state dominated by the edge effect.

The main excited state of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° at the secondary absorption peak is S53. TDM shows that there are two regions with higher transition density on the two layers of graphene on the main diagonal which is corresponding to the two regions where the isosurfaces on the electron-hole pair density map are gathered. The distribution of the hole-electron isosurface is completely symmetric. The distribution position of the symmetry axis is exactly the area where the BLG hole-electron isosurface dissipates, refer Fig. 4 (a), this is due to the further enhancement of the local excitation between the layers. The small transition density on both sides of the main diagonal of the TDM represents the weak charge transfer excitation in the BLG. When the twisted angle changes into 32.2°, the main excited state of the secondary absorption peak is also S53, and the degree of electronic excitation is very close, both of them are a mixture of inter-layer local excitation and intra-layer charge transfer excitation, it is interesting that the hole-electron isosurfaces on both sides gather and distribute exactly symmetrically, refer Fig. 4(b). The secondary absorption peak of Z-TwBLG-38.2° is solely contributed by S57. Compared with the main excited states of the secondary absorption peaks of the previous two twisted angles, the electron transition characteristic is still the inter-layer localized excitation dominated by edge hole-electron aggregation, the difference is that the direction of the symmetry axis of the hole-electron isosurface is different, refer Fig. 4(c). The other excited state in the secondary peak of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° and Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2° is S49, refer Fig. 5 (a and b), they all belong to the local excitation between the layers and the weak charge transfer excitation in the layers. It is similar to the main excited state S53 that the direction of the hole-electron isosurface distribution is still symmetrical. The secondary peak of Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2°, only includes S57. This is because this system has a completely different Moiré compared with the other two twisted angles, MS will cause changes in the energy level of BLG, thereby affecting the electronic transition behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The physical mechanism of hole-electron anti-symmetric distribution
ce:section.ce:para: In order to reveal the source of significant differences in the excited state hole-electron distribution positions of Z-TwBLG-QDs with MS under three twisted angles, the PDOS of the three systems in different regions was analyzed. The position of electron-hole pair density distribution depends on the transition dipole moment. The hole-electron isosurface of the antisymmetric distribution indicates that the twisted angle leads to the antisymmetric distribution of Z-TwBLG-QDs transition dipole moment. We divide Z-TwBLG-QDs into different fragments according to the distribution of the hole-electron isosurface, define the fragments according to atoms, and add all the basis functions on the atoms to the fragments. The defined intervals of fragment 1 and fragment 2 are adjacent to each other, representing the area where electrons and holes gather at the edge of the graphene, fragment 3 and fragment 4 define the interval position relative to each other, representing the area where a small amount of electrons and holes are distributed at the edge of the graphene, the definition of fragment 5 is the area where hole-electron dissipated inside the graphene, refer Fig. 6 (a, b and c). The lower part is the PDOS diagram after normalizing the three systems, the PDOS integral curves of these segments are marked with the same color. Near the energy of −2.8 eV, the PDOS of Z-TwBLG-27.8° shows that the peak value of the blue curve is greater than the peak value of the red curve, while the PDOS of Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.8° shows that the peak value of the red curve is greater than the peak value of the blue curve. That is to say, the contribution of the fragment orbitals in the adjacent regions has changed oppositely.

According to the Mulliken division, the sum of the orbital contributions of the atomic basis function is the contribution of the atom to the transition dipole moment. The orbital alternation will cause a significant change in the transition dipole moment, resulting in the transition out of the anti-symmetric charge density distribution which leads to the phenomenon of symmetrical distribution of the charge density on both sides of graphene in the excited state. In fact, because the Moiré of these two systems have different rotation directions, the contribution of their fragment orbital is derived from the complementary twisted angles of the period. Compared with the previous two systems, the PDOS of Z-TwBLG-QDs-38.2°, only changes the peak value of the PDOS curve representing the edge of the graphene with the same hole-electron distribution characteristics. This change in orbital contribution is not symmetrical, so the direction of hole-electron edge aggregation and distribution at this twisted angle is completely different from the above two systems.

Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° contains two paths in the two-photon excited state S26 of the main absorption peak, the intermediate state of the first path is S15 whose first transition process is composed of the transition from the ground state to S15, the TDM shows that the transition density of the first transition is mainly distributed on both sides of the main diagonal, the brightness of the transition density on the basis function representing the edge of graphene is significantly greater than the transition density on the basis function representing the internal graphene, which is consistent with the characteristic that the hole-electron isosurface in the electron-hole pair density is mainly distributed on the edge of Z-TwBLG-QDs, refer Fig. 7 (a), and the edge effect is obvious, explain that first step transition is the inter-layer local excitation dominated by the edge. The second step transition is the transition from excited state to excited state. From the perspective of TDM, the transition density of the basis functions representing the edges of the two BLG on the main diagonal changes from a uniform distribution to a concentrated block distribution, corresponding to the electron-hole pair density, electrons gather towards the edge. There are evenly distributed holes in the graphene due to the reduction of electrons, refer Fig. 7(b), and the distribution positions of electrons and holes on the bottom and top graphene sheets are exactly complementary, refer to side view of Fig. 7(b). This obvious separation of holes and electrons represents intra-molecular charge transfer, the electronic transition behavior is very different from the first transition. The value of the transition dipole moment shows that the second transition is greater than the first one, so the two-photon transition of this excited state has good availability. The intermediate state of the other path of S26 is S16, TDM shows that the first transition is further concentrated on the main diagonal transition density. It shows that the inter-layer local excitation at the edge becomes stronger, refer Fig. 7(d). The TDM of the second step transition shows that the transition density on the main diagonal decreases, and the edge effect weakened from the point of view of charge density. Some of the electrons at the edge are transferred to the inside of the grapheme. The hole-electron distributions of the underlying graphene and the top graphene are still complementary, but the whole is still charge transfer excitation, refer Fig. 7(e). Combined with one-step transition, it can be found that the two-photon transition characteristics of the two paths are very close. Therefore, the excitation characteristics of S26 are changed from the inter-layer local excitation of the first transition to the second step of intra-layer charge transfer excitation.

The two-photon contribution of the shoulder peak comes from S31. Its two-photon transition has the same two paths as S26. The first intermediate state is S15, and the transition characteristics from the ground state to S15 have been discussed. TDM shows that the transition density of the second step is evenly distributed on the main diagonal and on both sides, the electron-hole pair density shows that the hole-electron isosurface at the edge of the graphene is transferred to the Moiré. The area where the internal hole isosurface and the electron isosurface connect are distributed in a band with the edge effect disappearing, and the local excitation characteristic is very obvious, refer Fig. 7(c). The other intermediate state of S31 is S16, and the excitation characteristics of the second transition are the same as S15 to S31, refer Fig. 7(f). When the twisted angle is 32.2° the two-photon transition of the main absorption peak of Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2° consists of two intermediate states S16 and S15. Compared with Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8°, the intermediate state of the first path is S16, so the two-photon transition paths of these two systems are exactly opposite. From the perspective of TDM and electron-hole pair density, the two-photon excitation characteristics of the first path are the same as those of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° in the two paths, refer Fig. 8 (a-f). In fact, when the twisted angle is 27.8°, the product of the dipole moments in the two-step transition, the intermediate state S15 is greater than S16. When the twisted angle is 32.2°, the situation is reversed, refer Table 1 . It is the source of the reverse symmetry of their transition path. When the twisted angle is 38.2°, the main absorption peak is contributed by S25 and S31. The two-photon transition of S25 is also a two-step transition, and the intermediate state of the first path is S20. TDM shows that the first transition belongs to local excitation. The hole-electron isosurface shows obvious edge effect, refer Fig. 9 (a), and the second step transition belongs to the charge transfer within the layer, refer Fig. 9(b).

The two-photon transition properties of the second path are very close to the first path. However, there is a slight difference in the transition density on the main diagonal, which also belongs to the charge transfer within the layer, refer Fig. 9(d and e). The two-photon excited state S31 at the shoulder is a two-step transition. Compared with S25, it has the two paths as well. Since the excitation characteristics of the first transition are the same, and the second transition of path I belongs to local excitation from the perspective of TDM. The transition density is very small on the underlying graphene. The electron-hole pair density shows that the hole-electron isosurface on the underlying layer of graphene is much smaller than the top layer, but the distribution positions of electrons and holes are still symmetrical, refer Fig. 9(c).

The two-photon transition of the secondary absorption peak of Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8° is mainly contributed by the two excited states S42 and S40. The two paths of the two-photon transition are the same as the main absorption peak, and the first transition refers to the previous discussion. Fig. 10 (a and c) is the TDM and electron-hole pair density of the second transition in the two transition paths of S40. TDM shows that the transition density on the main diagonal of path II is greater than that of path I. From the perspective of electron-hole pair density, the degree of hole-electron separation becomes greater, but it is not completely separated. The internal part still belongs to local excitation, and the edge effect is weakened. The excitation characteristics of the two paths of the other two-photon excited state S42 are the same as S40, refer Fig. 10(b and d). The TDM of the second transition shows that the transition density on the main diagonal spreads from the atomic basis function representing the edge to the internal atomic basis function. From the point of view of charge density, the edge effect has completely disappeared, and there is an accumulation of hole-electron isosurfaces at the center of the system.

When the twisted angle is 32.2°, the two-photon transition characteristic of the secondary absorption peak has the opposite transition path compared with Z-TwBLG-QDs-27.8°, but the excitation characteristics are basically the same, refer Fig. 11 (a-d). When the twisted angle is 38.2°, the two paths of the two-photon transition of the secondary absorption peak are consistent with the main absorption peak, and the first transition has been discussed. The second transition of the intermediate state S20 of the shoulder excited state S38 is dominated by the edge local excitation, and the edge effect is very obvious. Another notable feature is that there is almost no transition density on the basis function of the top graphene, and the electron-hole pair density proves that the hole-electron isosurface of the underlying layer is much larger than that of the top layer, refer Fig. 12 (a and c).

The other two-photon excited state is S54. Compared with the shoulder peaks, TDM shows that a part of the transition density of the atomic basis functions on the underlying graphene diffuses to the top layer. From the perspective of electron-hole pair density, the hole-electron isosurface on the underlying graphene is still larger than the top layer, and the overall charge density is less than the shoulder peak, indicating that the two-photon transition from S38 to S54 is a process of reducing the edge effect, refer Fig. 12(b and d). The above discussion shows that the two-photon transition characteristics of the twisted BLG depend on the MS. When the twisted angle period is complementary. The MS of two systems are D-D pattern, and the rotation direction is different. At this time, their two-step transition paths are opposite. Because the intermediate states of the two paths are adjacent, and the transition dipole moments are very close. The two-photon excitation characteristics only have a small difference in the degree of electronic transition. The transition process of the two-photon transitions of the main absorption peak is from the intra-layer local excitation of the shoulder excited state to the intra-layer charge transfer of the main excited state. But the two-photon transition of the secondary absorption peak is the process of weakening the local excitation within the layer, and the distinguishing feature is the disappearance of the edge effect. When the twisted angle is 38.2°, the structure of the MS changes. The two-photon transition of the main absorption peak changes from the local excitation of the shoulder peak to the intra-layer charge transfer excitation of the main excited state. Its distinguishing feature is that the edge effect is very obvious. The distribution of the transition density of the shoulder excited state is different on the underlying graphene and the top graphene. The two excited states of the secondary absorption peak belong to the local excitation, and the edge effect weakens when the shoulder excited state changes to the main excited state.

The analysis of OPA and TPA shows that the edge effect of Z-TwBLG-QDs based on the MS in the excited state comes from the change of electronic transition behavior. In order to analyze the physical mechanism of the significant change in the electronic transition behavior under the Moiré,We investigated the induced current of BLG induced by an applied magnetic field. The induced current of the cyclic conjugate system is in the form of a ring, and the direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular to the BLG plane from the inside to the outside, refer Fig. 13 (a). When the twisted angle is 27.8°, the induced current is in a significant ring shape, and the red round arrow is the direction of the system ring current. The current isosurface in the yellow area is mainly distributed on the edge of the BLG. Combined with the side view below, it is found that the edge loop current induced by the magnetic field is significantly different from the internal induced current. The current isosurface is not only distributed on the edge, but also in the direction of the vertical plane, refer Fig. 13(b). This is due to the difference between the
s
p 2
hybrid orbitals inside and at the edges of graphene, which makes the edges more conjugated and has stronger delocalization. When the twisted angle is 32.2°, the MS of two system are D-D pattern while the rotation direction of the MS changed. There is a large current isosurface distribution on the edge and inside, refer Fig. 13(c). When the twisted angle is 38.2°, the MS of two system are D-S pattern, and the system induced current distribution characteristics is similar to Z-TwBLG-QDs-32.2°, refer Fig. 13(d). It shows that the direction and shape of the Moiré pattern will have a significant impact on the aromaticity of Z-TwBLG-QDs. Due to the aromaticity comes from the special electronic arrangement of the molecular orbital of the system, it will inevitably cause the change of electronic behavior, which is the fundamental reason for the change of the electronic transition behavior of the system under different twisted angles.

This paper uses a variety of first-principles calculations and analysis methods to study the spectrum of OPA, TPA, PDOS and magnetic induction current density of Z-TwBLG-QDs with different twisted angles, and proposes the regulation mechanism of two kinds of MS pattern on the electronic transition behavior of Z-TwBLG-QDs. The TDM and electron-hole pair density in spectral analysis reflect the linear and nonlinear optical properties of the three systems. Firstly, the OPA indicates that the D-D pattern of MS has similar electronic properties, so their electronic excitation properties are also consistent, the anti-symmetric distribution of electron-hole pairs is derived from the MS of D-D pattern where the atomic orbital contribution of the fragments in the adjacent area alternates. The MS of D-S pattern will significantly change the absorption intensity and peak position of the absorption spectrum. The mechanism of the MS on the TPA is achieved by adjusting the TPA cross section, which is also the main reason for the change of the TPA spectrum and the boundary effect of Z-TwBLG-QDs is regulated by the twisted angle. The magnetic induction current density shows that the MS can significantly change the aromaticity of the system, thereby affecting the electron configuration of the molecular orbital of the system, leading to a change in the electronic transition behavior.
Xiangtao Chen: Software, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Chen Lu: Software. Li Wang: Formal analysis, Resources. Jingang Wang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Scientific research fund project of Educational Department of Liaoning Province (No. L2019028). Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities and talent scientific research fund of LSHU (No. 2018XJJ-007).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.120894.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
